en.extract:
The white-browed shama (Copsychus luzoniensis) is a species of bird in the family Muscicapidae.
It is endemic to the Philippines found only on the islands of Luzon,Marinduque and the Polillo Islands. The Visayan shama (C. superciliaris), formerly considered a subspecies, was split as a distinct species in 2021.


Description and taxonomy

They exhibit sexual dimorphism in which females are paler with a gray chest and face with brown head, back and wings.


Subspecies
Three subspecies are recognized:

C. l. luzoniensis - Found on Luzon and Catanduanes
C. l. parvimaculatus - Found on the Polillo Islands; darker rump and has lesser extent of white on its tail tip
C. l. shemleyi - Found on Marinduque; darker rump and has greater extent of white on its tail tip
These subspecies are poorly differentiated and likely will be lumped in the future.


Ecology and behavior
Presumed to feed on small insects and larvae. Typically found in pairs foraging in the dense and lower foliage. This species is believed to be territorial. 
Breeding occurs in April and may. Nest is described as cub of grass and dry leaves. Lays 2 pale green eggs with reddish brown spots. 


Habitat and conservation status
Found in primary forest, mature secondary forest typically in below 1,000 m (3,280 ft) asl. It is believed to be generally common. 
This species has been assessed as Least-concern species by the International Union for Conservation of Nature as it remains locally common and relatively tolerant to disturbed habitats. This species is still believed to be on the decline due to the extensive habitat clearance. This species' main threat is habitat loss with wholesale clearance of forest habitats as a result of logging, agricultural conversion and mining activities occurring within the range. This species is found in a few protected areas including Bataan National Park, Northern Sierra Madre Natural Park, Angat Watershed Forest Reserve, Mount Makiling, Kalbario–Patapat Natural Park, Bicol Natural Park and Bulusan Volcano Natural Park but actual protection from deforestation is still lax. 


References

The '''white-browed shama''' ('''''Copsychus luzoniensis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Muscicapidae]].
It is [[endemic]] to the [[Philippines]] found only on the islands of [[Luzon]],[[Marinduque]] and the [[Polillo Islands]]. The [[Visayan shama]] (''C. superciliaris''), formerly considered a subspecies, was split as a distinct species in 2021.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Species Updates – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/species-updates/|access-date=2021-05-27|language=en-US}}</ref>

== Description and taxonomy ==
[[File:Copsychus luzoniensis 1832.jpg|left|thumb|208x208px|An illustration]]
They exhibit [[sexual dimorphism]] in which females are paler with a gray chest and face with brown head, back and wings.<ref name=":0">{{Cite taxon | BOW |last1=Collar |first1=Nigel |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=White-browed Shama (''Copsychus luzoniensis'')|version=1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/whbsha1/cur/introduction |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.whbsha1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Three [[subspecies]] are recognized:

* ''C. l. luzoniensis'' - Found on [[Luzon]] and [[Catanduanes]]
* ''C. l. parvimaculatus'' - Found on the [[Polillo Islands]]; darker rump and has lesser extent of white on its tail tip
* ''C. l. shemleyi'' - Found on [[Marinduque]]; darker rump and has greater extent of white on its tail tip

These subspecies are poorly differentiated and likely will be lumped in the future.

== Ecology and behavior ==
Presumed to feed on small insects and larvae. Typically found in pairs foraging in the dense and lower foliage. This species is believed to be territorial. 

Breeding occurs in April and may. Nest is described as cub of grass and dry leaves. Lays 2 pale green eggs with reddish brown spots. 

== Habitat and conservation status ==
Found in primary forest, mature secondary forest typically in below {{cvt|1000|m|-1}} asl. It is believed to be generally common. 

This species has been assessed as [[Least-concern species]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] as it remains locally common and relatively tolerant to disturbed habitats. This species is still believed to be on the decline due to the extensive habitat clearance. This species' main threat is [[habitat loss]] with wholesale clearance of forest habitats as a result of logging, agricultural conversion and mining activities occurring within the range. This species is found in a few protected areas including [[Bataan National Park]], [[Northern Sierra Madre Natural Park]], [[Angat Watershed Forest Reserve]], [[Mount Makiling]], [[Kalbario–Patapat Natural Park]], [[Bicol Natural Park]] and [[Bulusan Volcano Natural Park]] but actual protection from deforestation is still lax.<ref name=":0" />
